text:
The Australian 2024 Olympics Chef de mission has raised serious concerns with the 2024 Paris Opening Ceremony after one huge change to the iconic event.

Fewer than a quarter of Australia’s athletes will participate in the Olympic Games opening ceremony in Paris on Friday night amid concerns about a lack of toilets on the boats that will transport them down the River Seine.

Chef de mission Anna Meares has also reinforced messages around athlete safety following the alleged gang rape of an Australian woman at the weekend and the attempted mugging of two Nine journalists.

Australia has 460 athletes competing in the Paris Games, but Meares said just 80 were expected to take part in the opening ceremony.

“At the moment we’ve got approximately 200 spaces where we’ve got Australians going, we’re still looking at about 80 athletes attending and that’s probably just over 100 staff in attendance as well,” she said.

For the first time in Olympic history, the Paris opening ceremony will not take place in a stadium.

Instead, the parade of athletes will take place on boats that will travel along the River Seine through the heart of Paris.

However, concerns have been raised that many of the boats will not have toilets, a potentially problematic issue given athletes will by hydrating ahead of their events.

Meares has already raised the issue with Olympics organisers and is still waiting for a response.

“No, we don’t know (about the toilets). I’m waiting on that information from the briefing this morning,” Meares said on Wednesday.

Australian boxers Teremoana Teremoana and Caitlin Parker said they would be participating in the ceremony.

“I’ll be going, the past few weeks I’ve been doing some shoulder exercises so I can do a bit of this (practices waving) when I’m on the boat,” Teremoana said.

“I think it will be a pretty cool experience because it’s on a boat.  I think it’s a bit different from walking around.  At least I don’t have to walk I can just stand still and wave around, so I’m happy with that.”

Parker said she would definitely be going.

“The opening ceremony is something I’ve looked forward to since I was a kid,” she said.

“In Tokyo we didn’t get to see too much, we were straight through the stadium and back on the bus to the village so I’m very excited about this opening ceremony I know France will put on an amazing show and I think it’s so magical.”

But Tokyo bronze medallist Harry Garside was unsure if he would be participating.

“Boxing is a making-weight sport so it depends how much you’re struggling to make weight and stuff like that,” he said.

“Once the draw is done it starts to get a little bit more real I think I’ll make the decision but at the moment I’m not too sure.”

Meares said athletes had been given advice around their safety following the alleged gang rape of an Australian woman in Paris at the weekend, and the attempted mugging of two Nine journalists.

“Our advice stays as standard and we also have to appreciate that we are in one of the biggest cities in the world and crime is unfortunately not void in city life and life in general, it doesn’t matter what city you are in,” she said.

Garside said he was not too concerned about safety.

“I just want to say merci, merci, merci, to the French.  Thank you so much for having us,” he said.

“It’s such a big commitment.  Like imagine how annoying it would be, the Paris locals are just trying to go about their life, pay their bills, do the things that we’re all trying to do when we’re back home in our day-to-day life so a massive thank you to the French people for having us.”
